transcript: Hamburger History, briefly. The history of the hamburger is a bit nebulous, but for fun, we can go back as far as the Mongols of the 13th century. Apparently, they used to shove bits of raw mutton under their saddles as they rode, tenderizing their dinner. Soon after, the Russians adopted a minced raw beef, added spices, and eventually the dish made its way all the way to Germany 300 years later, where chopped beef was served cooked or raw. Germans emigrating to the United States in the mid-19th century had to leave out of the port of Hamburg and would sometimes have to wait months for passage. It would have been at this point that they developed the fondness for a local dish called the Hamburg steak, which was chopped, cooked beef served on a plate with potatoes and gravy. Upon arriving in New York City, the Germans found that their familiar food also made its way to America and was already hugely popular, from high-end restaurants like Del Monaco's to food carts all over lower Manhattan. News of the hamburger steak spread and soon it was available all across America. Now things get interesting. It took another 20 to 30 years for the hamburger steak to be served between two slices of bread. All of the claims to the invention of the traditional hamburger are a bit shaky and happened around the same time, 1885 to 1900. Most of the claims come from transient state fair vendors that reportedly were looking for a way for fairgoers to eat their meatballs on the go. Fletch Davis, the Menchez brothers, Charlie Negrin, and others have laid claim to the invention. However, the first established food cart to sell something named a hamburger sandwich was Louie's Lunch in New Haven, Connecticut in the year 1900. Probably the single most important moment in modern hamburger history was unquestionably the standardization of the hamburger sandwich was louie's lunch in new haven connecticut in the year 1900 probably the single most important moment in modern hamburger history was unquestionably the standardization of the hamburger bun by white castle in 1920 from that point on a patty between two buns became recognized around the world as a distinctly american dish called the hamburger © BF-WATCH TV 2021
